document.source_title: How the Hormuz crisis keeps disrupting kitchens, ports and paychecks
document.summary: The ongoing crisis in the Strait of Hormuz is causing significant disruptions to global economies, particularly in Asia and developing regions. Rising fuel prices and disrupted shipping routes are leading to increased inflation and financial uncertainty. The International Labour Organization warns that the crisis could reshape labor markets and lead to a decline in global working hours and incomes, with vulnerable communities facing severe challenges.
key_takeaways: The Strait of Hormuz is crucial for global oil and gas supplies, handling about 20 million barrels a day.
Ship traffic has drastically decreased, with only 2 to 16 vessels per day compared to over 100 before the crisis.
Global growth is projected to slow to 2.5% in 2026, significantly below pre-pandemic levels.
Inflation in Asia is rising, with countries like Laos and Pakistan experiencing sharp increases in consumer prices.
The crisis is expected to lead to a decline in global working hours by 0.5% in 2026 and 1.1% in 2027.
Real labor incomes could decline by up to $3 trillion globally by 2027, affecting vulnerable regions the most.
document.why_it_matters: The research highlights the interconnectedness of global economies and the potential long-term impacts of geopolitical crises on labor markets and economic stability, emphasizing the need for resilience in the face of such disruptions.

claims.claim_text: The Strait of Hormuz handles roughly one-fifth of global oil supplies.
claims.evidence_text: Before the latest tensions, the Strait of Hormuz handled roughly one-fifth of global oil supplies – around 20 million barrels a day.

claims.claim_text: Global growth is projected to slow to 2.5% in 2026.
claims.evidence_text: Global growth is now projected to slow to 2.5 per cent in 2026 – well below pre-pandemic levels.

claims.claim_text: Inflation in Laos rose from 6.2% in February to over 10% in April.
claims.evidence_text: In Lao People’s Democratic Republic, headline inflation – which measures overall consumer prices – rose from 6.2 per cent in February to more than 10 per cent in April.

claims.claim_text: The crisis could lead to a decline in global working hours by 0.5% in 2026.
claims.evidence_text: Under one scenario modelled by the agency – in which oil prices remain around 50 per cent above their early 2026 average – global working hours could fall by 0.5 per cent this year.

entities.name: UNCTAD
entities.description: The UN trade and development body that released the report on global growth.
entities.evidence_text: according to a report released on Tuesday by economists at UNCTAD, the UN trade and development body.

entities.name: ESCAP
entities.description: The UN regional economic commission that reported on the economic outlook for Asia.
entities.evidence_text: according to the UN regional economic commission, ESCAP.

entities.name: International Labour Organization (ILO)
entities.description: The agency warning about the impact of the crisis on jobs and wages.
entities.evidence_text: The International Labour Organization (ILO) warned that the crisis was increasingly affecting jobs, wages and working conditions worldwide.

entities.name: Myanmar
entities.description: A country facing severe economic challenges due to rising fuel prices.
entities.evidence_text: Families in Myanmar have been hit hard by rising prices, with the most vulnerable struggling to meet their daily needs.

events.event: UN agencies warn about rising fuel prices and economic pressure.
events.evidence_text: with UN agencies warning that rising fuel prices, disrupted shipping routes and growing financial uncertainty are placing mounting pressure on economies.
In Lao People’s Democratic Republic, headline inflation – which measures overall consumer prices – rose from 6.2 per cent in February to more than 10 per cent in April.
Pakistan also saw inflation jump from 7.3 per cent in March to 10.9 per cent in April.

citations.title: How the Hormuz crisis keeps disrupting kitchens, ports and paychecks

source_credibility.reason: The source is a UN news outlet, providing credible information on global economic issues.

geolocation.name: Strait of Hormuz
geolocation.evidence_text: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow but vital shipping route linking the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the wider Arabian Sea.
